questions.question: A cycle on n vertices is isomorphic to its complement. The value of n is _____.
questions.to_find: The number of vertices n for which the cycle C_n is self-complementary

understand.plain: A cycle C_n has exactly n edges. Its complement (the same n vertices, but with edges exactly where C_n had none) must look like C_n again if C_n is isomorphic to its complement. Two graphs that are isomorphic have the same number of edges, so we just need the cycle and its complement to carry the same edge count. Solving that equation pins n down to a single value, and we then confirm the complement really is a cycle.

keywords.explain: The complement G^c has the same vertices as G, and two vertices are joined in G^c exactly when they are NOT joined in G.
keywords.example: For 5 vertices the complete graph K_5 has 10 edges, so any graph with e edges has 10 - e edges in its complement.

keywords.explain: A graph isomorphic to its own complement; necessarily its edge count equals half of C(n,2).
keywords.example: C_5 is self-complementary: its complement is again a 5-cycle (the pentagram).

keywords.explain: A connected 2-regular graph on n vertices with exactly n edges, each vertex joined to its two neighbours.
keywords.example: C_5 is a pentagon with 5 vertices and 5 edges.

5</text><line class='edge' x1='300' y1='60' x2='320' y2='155' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='320' y1='155' x2='250' y2='95' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='250' y1='95' x2='350' y2='95' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='350' y1='95' x2='270' y2='155' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='155' x2='300' y2='60' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='300' cy='60' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='350' cy='95' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='320' cy='155' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='270' cy='155' r='9' class='hi'/><circle cx='250' cy='95' r='9' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='195' class='sub no'>complement = C_5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: On the left a pentagon C_5 with 5 edges; on the right its complement drawn as a pentagram, which is again a 5-cycle, showing C_5 is self-complementary.

given.aim: Find n so that the cycle C_n is isomorphic to its complement.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices of the cycle.
terms.example: We test which n makes C_n self-complementary.
terms.connects: Determines both the edge count n and the complement's edge count.

terms.meaning: A cycle on n vertices has exactly n edges.
terms.connects: Left side of the edge-balance equation.

terms.meaning: C(n,2) - n, the edges of K_n not used by the cycle.
terms.connects: Must equal n for an isomorphism to be possible.

given.plan: Set the cycle's edge count equal to its complement's edge count, solve the resulting equation for n, then confirm the complement is actually a cycle.

steps.title: Balance the edge counts
steps.formula_raw: edges(G) = edges(G^c)  =>  n = C(n,2) - n = n(n-1)/2 - n
steps.apply: C_n has n edges; its complement has C(n,2) - n edges. Isomorphic graphs have equal edge counts, so n = n(n-1)/2 - n.
steps.note: This is a necessary condition; equal edge counts are required (though not by themselves sufficient).

steps.title: Solve for n and verify
steps.apply: From n = n(n-1)/2 - n we get 2n = n(n-1)/2, so 4n = n(n-1), giving n - 1 = 4 and n = 5. For n = 5 the complement of C_5 is the pentagram, which is itself a 5-cycle, so C_5 is indeed isomorphic to its complement.
steps.note: n = 5 is the unique positive answer; C_5 is the smallest self-complementary cycle.

solution.result: The cycle C_n is isomorphic to its complement when n = 5.

questions.formula_note: The self-complementary edge condition edges(G) = (1/2)C(n,2) applied to a cycle (n edges) forces n = 5, and C_5's complement is verifiably another 5-cycle.

questions.question: The following simple undirected graph is referred to as the Petersen graph. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? A. The chromatic number of the graph is 3. B. The graph has a Hamiltonian path. C. The following graph is isomorphic to the Petersen graph. D. The size of the largest independent set of the given graph is 3. (A subset of vertices of a graph form an independent set if no two vertices of the subset are adjacent.)
questions.to_find: Which statements about the Petersen graph are TRUE

understand.plain: The Petersen graph is the famous 3-regular graph on 10 vertices (an outer pentagon, an inner pentagram, and spokes joining them). We check each claim against its well-known properties: it needs 3 colours (chromatic number 3), it has a Hamiltonian path even though it has no Hamiltonian cycle, the second drawing is just another standard layout of the same graph, and its independence number is 4, not 3. So A, B and C are true while D is false.

keywords.explain: The fewest colours needed to colour the vertices so that adjacent vertices differ in colour.
keywords.example: The Petersen graph is not bipartite (it has 5-cycles) yet 3 colours suffice, so its chromatic number is 3.

keywords.explain: A Hamiltonian path visits every vertex exactly once; a Hamiltonian cycle does the same and returns to the start. The Petersen graph has the former but not the latter.
keywords.example: Walking the 10 Petersen vertices in a single open trail uses every vertex once, giving a Hamiltonian path.

keywords.explain: An independent set has no two adjacent vertices; the independence number is the size of the largest such set.
keywords.example: The Petersen graph has independence number 4 (e.g. the four vertices of a suitable layout), so claim D's value 3 is too small.

understand.visual_alt: The Petersen graph: an outer pentagon of 5 vertices joined by spokes to an inner pentagram of 5 vertices, every vertex of degree 3.

given.aim: Decide which of the four statements about the Petersen graph hold.

terms.meaning: A 3-regular simple graph on 10 vertices and 15 edges (outer 5-cycle, inner 5-cycle as pentagram, 5 spokes).
terms.example: Girth 5, vertex- and edge-transitive, no triangles.
terms.connects: Its standard invariants decide every statement.

terms.meaning: Minimum colours for a proper colouring of the Petersen graph.
terms.example: Not 2 (odd cycles exist), but 3 colours suffice.

terms.meaning: Largest set of mutually non-adjacent vertices in the Petersen graph.
terms.example: Four pairwise non-adjacent vertices can be chosen.

given.plan: Recall the standard invariants of the Petersen graph and test each statement A-D against them.

steps.title: Statements A, B, C are true
steps.formula_raw: Petersen: chi = 3, has Hamiltonian path (but no Hamiltonian cycle), unique up to isomorphism
steps.apply: A: the Petersen graph is non-bipartite (it has 5-cycles) yet 3-colourable, so its chromatic number is 3 - TRUE. B: it admits a path through all 10 vertices, so it has a Hamiltonian path - TRUE. C: the second drawing is another standard layout of the same 3-regular, girth-5 graph; it is isomorphic to the Petersen graph - TRUE.
steps.note: Note the Petersen graph famously has NO Hamiltonian cycle, but the question only asks about a Hamiltonian path.

steps.title: Statement D is false
steps.apply: The largest independent set of the Petersen graph has 4 vertices (its independence number is 4), so the claim that the largest independent set has size 3 is too small - FALSE.
steps.note: alpha = 4 pairs with the clique cover / fractional structure; D understates it by one.

solution.result: Statements A, B and C are true while D is false; the answer is A, B, C.

questions.formula_note: The fixed invariants of the Petersen graph (chi = 3, Hamiltonian path exists, independence number 4) make A, B, C true and D false.

questions.question: How many perfect matchings are there in a complete graph of 6 vertices? A. 15 B. 24 C. 30 D. 60
questions.to_find: Number of perfect matchings in the complete graph K_6

understand.plain: A perfect matching pairs up all the vertices into disjoint edges, so on 6 vertices we form 3 pairs that together cover everyone. Counting the ways to split 6 labelled vertices into 3 unordered pairs is a standard count: pair off one vertex at a time, then divide out the order of the pairs. The result for K_6 is 15.

keywords.explain: A set of edges that covers every vertex exactly once (no vertex is left out, no two edges share a vertex).
keywords.example: On 6 vertices a perfect matching is 3 disjoint edges covering all 6.

keywords.explain: Every pair of the 6 vertices is joined by an edge, so any pairing is a valid matching.
keywords.example: K_6 has C(6,2) = 15 edges, and every potential pair is usable.

keywords.explain: The number of perfect matchings of 2m labelled vertices is (2m-1)!! = (2m)!/(2^m m!).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='378' height='198' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='28' class='tag ink'>One perfect matching of K_6: 3 disjoint edges</text><line class='edge' x1='60' y1='90' x2='140' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='90' x2='240' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='260' y1='90' x2='340' y2='90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='140' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='260' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><circle cx='340' cy='90' r='12' class='hi'/><text x='55' y='95' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='135' y='95' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='155' y='95' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='235' y='95' class='sub ink'>4</text><text x='255' y='95' class='sub ink'>5</text><text x='335' y='95' class='sub ink'>6</text><text x='80' y='160' class='lbl ok'>count = 5 x 3 x 1 = 15</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: One perfect matching of K_6 shown as three disjoint edges {1-2, 3-4, 5-6}; the total number of such matchings is 5 x 3 x 1 = 15.

given.aim: Count the perfect matchings of the complete graph on 6 vertices.

terms.meaning: Number of vertices to be paired; m = 3 pairs (edges) form each matching.
terms.connects: Plugged into the double-factorial / pairing formula.

terms.meaning: Complete graph: every pair of vertices is adjacent, so all pairings are allowed.
terms.example: Has 15 edges; any 3 disjoint edges form a matching.
terms.connects: Guarantees every pairing is a valid perfect matching.

terms.meaning: Number of ways to partition the 6 vertices into 3 unordered disjoint pairs.

given.plan: Use the formula for the number of perfect matchings of 2m labelled vertices, (2m)!/(2^m m!), with 2m = 6.

steps.title: State the matching count formula
steps.apply: For K_6 we have 2m = 6, so m = 3 and the number of perfect matchings is 6!/(2^3 * 3!).
steps.note: Equivalently pick a partner for the first vertex (5 ways), then for the next free vertex (3 ways), then the last pair (1 way): 5 x 3 x 1.

steps.title: Evaluate for K_6
steps.apply: Compute 720 / 48 = 15, matching the double-factorial 5!! = 5 x 3 x 1 = 15.
steps.note: The distractors 30 and 60 forget to divide out the ordering of the 3 pairs.

solution.result: The complete graph K_6 has 15 perfect matchings; the answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The pairing count (2m)!/(2^m m!) with 2m = 6 gives 6!/48 = 15, i.e. 5 x 3 x 1, which is option A.

formulas.name: Edge Condition for a Self-Complementary Graph
formulas.whenToUse: When a graph is isomorphic to its complement (self-complementary); it must carry exactly half of all possible edges, which forces n = 0 or 1 (mod 4).

terms.means: the complement of G (edges exactly where G has none)

formulas.trap: Equal edge counts are necessary but not sufficient; you must still verify an actual isomorphism (for a cycle, check the complement is again a cycle).
formulas.reference: Graph theory; self-complementary graphs and the complement operation.

formulas.name: Standard Invariants of the Petersen Graph
formulas.latex: n=10,\ m=15,\ \text{3-regular},\ g=5,\ \chi=3,\ \alpha=4,\ \text{Ham. path but no Ham. cycle}
formulas.plain: Petersen graph: 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, girth 5, chromatic number 3, independence number 4, has a Hamiltonian path but no Hamiltonian cycle
formulas.whenToUse: Answering true/false statements about the Petersen graph's coloring, matching, Hamiltonicity, independence and isomorphism.
formulas.trap: The Petersen graph has a Hamiltonian PATH but NO Hamiltonian CYCLE; its independence number is 4 (not 3).
formulas.reference: Graph theory; the Petersen graph as the Kneser graph K(5,2).

formulas.name: Number of Perfect Matchings of K_{2m}
formulas.plain: number of perfect matchings of K_{2m} = (2m)!/(2^m * m!) = (2m-1)!!
formulas.whenToUse: Counting the ways to pair up 2m labelled vertices into m disjoint edges (a perfect matching) in a complete graph.

terms.means: number of edges in the matching = m pairs

formulas.trap: Divide by 2^m m! to remove the within-pair order and the order of the m pairs; forgetting this overcounts (e.g. gives 90 or 720 instead of 15).
formulas.reference: Combinatorics; pairings of a labelled set, double factorial.
